section.title: The Two Baskets of Figs
section.sectionRationale: A short, sharp vision reverses everyone's expectations: the exiles already carried off to Babylon, the ones who looked like the losers, are the good figs God regards for good, while those who remained in Jerusalem and Zedekiah's court, who seemed to have escaped judgment, are the bad figs marked for disaster.
section.thesis: God's favor does not follow appearances or circumstances; the exiles who seem cast off are the good figs He will watch over for good and give a new heart to know Him, while those who seem to have kept their place are the bad figs already appointed to ruin.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: the vision of two baskets set before the temple, the good figs identified as the exiles marked for restoration, and the bad figs identified as Zedekiah and the remnant marked for disaster.

units.label: The vision of two baskets
units.note: very good figs and very bad figs

verses.text: After Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon had carried away Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim king of Judah, as well as the officials of Judah and the craftsmen and metalsmiths from Jerusalem, and had brought them to Babylon, the LORD showed me two baskets of figs placed in front of the temple of the LORD.
One basket had very good figs, like those that ripen early, but the other basket contained very poor figs, so bad they could not be eaten.

“Jeremiah,” the LORD asked, “what do you see?” 
“Figs!” I replied. “The good figs are very good, but the bad figs are very bad, so bad they cannot be eaten.”

units.label: The good figs, a heart to know Me

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: ‘Like these good figs, so I regard as good the exiles from Judah, whom I have sent away from this place to the land of the Chaldeans.
I will keep My eyes on them for good and will return them to this land. I will build them up and not tear them down; I will plant them and not uproot them.
I will give them a {{lev|heart}} to {{yada|know}} Me, that I am the LORD. They will be My people, and I will be their God, for they will return to Me with all their heart.

units.label: The bad figs, appointed for disaster
units.note: Zedekiah and the remnant left behind

verses.text: But like the bad figs, so bad they cannot be eaten,’ says the LORD, ‘so will I deal with Zedekiah king of Judah, his officials, and the remnant of Jerusalem, those remaining in this land and those living in the land of Egypt.
I will make them a horror and an offense to all the kingdoms of the earth, a disgrace and an object of scorn, ridicule, and cursing wherever I have banished them.
And I will send against them sword and famine and plague, until they have perished from the land that I gave to them and their fathers.’”

lev.gloss: Heart, the seat of thought, will, and desire in Hebrew, not merely emotion. God promises to give the exiles a new lev to know Him, an inward transformation rather than only an outward change of circumstance, a promise that grows into the full new covenant language of Jeremiah 31:33.

yada.gloss: To know, in Hebrew a rich word for intimate, relational knowledge, not merely factual information. God's promise that the exiles will have a heart to yada Him describes covenant relationship restored from the inside, exactly what verse 16 of chapter 22 also calls knowing the LORD.

basic.context: After Babylon's first deportation of Judah's leaders and skilled workers, God shows Jeremiah two baskets of figs at the temple, one full of excellent figs, one full of figs so rotten they cannot be eaten. The shock of the vision is who is who: the exiles already carried off to Babylon, who looked like the ones who had lost everything, are the good figs God will watch over for good, while King Zedekiah and those who remained in the land, who seemed to have escaped judgment, are the bad figs marked for disaster.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a straightforward symbolic vision with its meaning explained directly in the text itself, no guesswork required, God states plainly which basket represents which group and why. The challenge for readers is resisting the natural assumption that whoever suffers most visibly must be under God's greatest judgment; this vision insists the opposite is often true, present suffering can be the very context of God's deepest future mercy.
basic.originalLanguages: The heart of the promise to the exiles is that God will give them a new lev, heart, to yada, know, Him, language of inward transformation and intimate relationship rather than mere external return to the land. This same pairing of heart and knowing God anticipates the fuller new covenant promise later in Jeremiah 31:33-34, where God writes His law on the heart itself.
basic.typology: The exile that looked like pure catastrophe becomes, in this vision, the very context of God's saving purpose, a pattern that anticipates how God works redemption through apparent defeat, most fully through the cross, where what looked like total loss became the means of salvation. The promise of a new heart to know God anticipates the new covenant of chapter 31 and its fulfillment in the Spirit's inward work through Christ.
basic.christ: The promise of a heart to know God, given specifically to a people in exile and disgrace, anticipates the new covenant Christ inaugurates, where God's Spirit writes His law within and gives believers the very knowledge of God this vision promises, Hebrews 8:10-11. Just as the exiles' apparent loss became the setting for God's deepest work of grace, Christ's own apparent defeat on the cross becomes the very means of the world's redemption.
basic.god: God's favor does not track with visible circumstances; He sees and values what human observers would overlook or dismiss, choosing the exiles, not those who remained comfortably in Jerusalem, as the objects of His restoring grace. Yet God is equally just, refusing to soften His verdict on Zedekiah and those who trusted their apparent safety instead of turning to Him.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 31:33 develops this same heart-transformation promise into the full new covenant. Hebrews 8:10-11 quotes that new covenant directly, showing its fulfillment in Christ. 1 Corinthians 1:27-29 shows God consistently choosing the lowly and despised to shame what seems strong. Romans 8:28 echoes the pattern that God works even hard circumstances for the good of those who love Him.
basic.oneStory: The two baskets of figs show that God's redemptive purposes often run precisely opposite to human appearances, choosing the exiled and apparently rejected as the ones He is quietly preparing for restoration and a new heart. That pattern reaches its fullest expression in Christ, whose apparent defeat on the cross became the very means by which God gives His people new hearts to truly know Him.

context.literary: This short vision report follows immediately after the review of Judah's failed kings and false prophets, functioning as a hinge that redirects hope away from the royal court in Jerusalem and toward the exiles in Babylon, a surprising reversal that reorients the rest of the book's expectations for where God's future lies.
context.historical: The vision is dated after Nebuchadnezzar's first deportation in 597 BC, which took King Jeconiah, the royal family, officials, and skilled craftsmen to Babylon, leaving Zedekiah installed as a puppet king over those who remained, precisely the two groups this vision addresses.
context.canonical: This vision anticipates and shapes the fuller promises of restoration developed later in Jeremiah's Book of Consolation, chapters 30-31, where the heart-transformation promised briefly here becomes the centerpiece of the new covenant.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To overturn the assumption, held by those who remained in Jerusalem, that surviving Babylon's first deportation was a sign of God's favor, and to redirect the nation's hope toward the exiles as the actual focus of God's future restoring work.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision describes a specific historical judgment on specific groups, the exiles of 597 BC versus Zedekiah's remnant; it is not prescribing that exile or hardship always signals divine favor in every case. The enduring principle is that God's favor cannot be read off of visible circumstances alone.

debates.question: Why would God favor the exiles, who suffered the trauma of deportation, over those who remained safely in the land?

views.label: Exile as disciplined grace, not mere punishment
views.case: The exiles' removal, though painful, put them in the place where God would work a new heart in them, verse 7, showing that hardship under God's hand can be the very context of His deepest transforming grace.

views.label: Remaining in Jerusalem as false security
views.case: Those who remained, especially under Zedekiah, presumed on their continued presence in the land as proof of God's favor, when in fact their persistence in the same sins that caused the first deportation only stored up greater judgment.

debates.bearing: Both observations converge on the same lesson: outward circumstances, whether suffering or apparent safety, are not reliable indicators of God's favor; only genuine turning to Him is.

study.originalLanguages: God's promise to the exiles centers on giving them a new lev, heart, to yada, know, Him, language describing inward transformation and intimate covenant relationship rather than merely a change of external circumstances. This pairing directly anticipates the fuller new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:33-34, where God's law is written on the heart itself, showing this vision as an early seed of that later, greater promise.

typology.type: The exiles' apparent loss becoming the setting for God's restoring grace, Jeremiah 24:5-7
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates how God consistently works redemption through apparent defeat, fulfilled supremely in the cross, where Christ's apparent loss became the means of the world's salvation.

typology.type: The promise of a new heart to know God, Jeremiah 24:7
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the new covenant Christ inaugurates, where God's Spirit writes His law within believers and gives them true knowledge of Him, Hebrews 8:10-11.

study.christ: The promise of a heart to truly know God, extended here to a people in the depths of exile and apparent disgrace, anticipates the new covenant Christ brings, in which God's Spirit accomplishes exactly this inward transformation, Hebrews 8:10-11. Just as the exiles' apparent catastrophe became the very context of God's deepest saving work, so Christ's own apparent defeat at the cross becomes the means of the world's redemption, the pattern of this vision fulfilled at the center of the gospel.
study.god: God's favor does not follow visible success or apparent security; He identifies with and works good for those the world would call losers, the exiles, while holding those who presumed on their continued comfort fully accountable. His justice and His mercy operate together here, refusing to spare Zedekiah's remnant from consequences while freely promising a new heart to a people who had lost everything else.

crossReferences.note: Develops the heart-transformation promised briefly here into the full new covenant, God's law written on the heart itself.
Quotes Jeremiah's new covenant directly, showing its fulfillment through Christ.
God consistently chooses what seems weak, lowly, and despised in the eyes of the world to accomplish His purposes.
Echoes the pattern that God works even painful circumstances for the good of those who love Him.

study.oneStory: The two baskets of figs reveal that God's saving purposes often run directly opposite to human appearances, choosing the exiled and apparently rejected as the very people He is preparing to give new hearts. That reversal reaches its climax in Christ, whose apparent defeat at the cross becomes the means by which God gives His people everywhere the new heart this vision first promised.

quiz.q: Who do the good figs represent in this vision?

options.text: The exiles already carried off to Babylon in the first deportation.
options.note: Verse 5 explicitly identifies the good figs as 'the exiles from Judah' sent to the land of the Chaldeans.

options.text: King Zedekiah and the officials who remained in Jerusalem.
options.note: This group is explicitly identified with the bad figs in verse 8.

options.text: The nations surrounding Judah.
options.note: The vision concerns two groups within Judah itself, not surrounding nations.

options.text: The priests specifically, as distinct from other exiles.
options.note: The good figs represent the exiles generally, not one professional group in particular.

quiz.q: What is surprising or reversing expectations about this vision?

options.text: Those who suffered the apparent loss of exile are marked for God's favor, while those who seemed to remain safely are marked for disaster.
options.note: This overturns the natural assumption that visible hardship signals God's disfavor and safety signals His approval.

options.text: Both baskets of figs turn out to represent the exact same group of people.
options.note: The vision explicitly distinguishes two separate groups with opposite fates.

options.text: The vision shows that all figs, good and bad, are eventually eaten.
options.note: The bad figs are explicitly described as inedible, and the two groups meet very different fates, not the same outcome.

options.text: It shows that Babylon itself will be shown mercy alongside Judah.
options.note: The vision concerns two groups within Judah, not Babylon's own fate.

quiz.q: What specific promise does God make to the good figs in verse 7?

options.text: He will give them a heart to know Him, and they will return to Him with all their heart.
options.note: This is an inward, relational transformation, not merely a change of external circumstance.

options.text: He will give them great material wealth immediately.
options.note: The stated promise centers on a transformed heart and restored relationship, not material wealth.

options.text: He will exempt them from all future hardship of any kind.
options.note: The promise concerns their ultimate restoration and heart transformation, not exemption from all difficulty.

options.text: He will make them rulers over the nations that exiled them.
options.note: The promise is about their return, their heart, and their relationship with God, not political rule over Babylon.

quiz.q: What does God pronounce over Zedekiah and the remnant left in the land?

options.text: He will make them a horror and disgrace among the nations and send sword, famine, and plague against them.
options.note: Verses 8 to 10 pronounce severe judgment on this group, in stark contrast to the promise given the exiles.

options.text: He promises them the same heart transformation given to the exiles.
options.note: This promise is given specifically to the good figs, the exiles, not to Zedekiah's remnant.

options.text: He offers them an immediate opportunity to repent and avoid all judgment.
options.note: The passage pronounces the verdict directly rather than extending a fresh offer within this vision.

options.text: He shows them no different treatment than the exiles at all.
options.note: The two groups are explicitly given opposite fates in this vision.

quiz.q: How does the promise in verse 7 anticipate later revelation?

options.text: It anticipates the fuller new covenant promise in Jeremiah 31, where God writes His law on the heart, ultimately fulfilled through Christ.
options.note: The language of a new heart to know God directly foreshadows Jeremiah 31:33-34 and its New Testament fulfillment.

options.text: It has no connection to any later promise in Scripture.
options.note: The heart-and-knowing language directly parallels and anticipates Jeremiah 31:33-34.

options.text: It anticipates only a return to strict ceremonial law-keeping.
options.note: The promise concerns inward transformation and relationship, not merely external ceremonial observance.

options.text: It anticipates judgment on the exiles rather than restoration.
options.note: The promise here is explicitly restorative and relational, not a further judgment on the exiles.

questions: The exiles who looked like the losers were actually the ones God was preparing for restoration. Where might you be misreading a hard season of your own life as God's disfavor rather than His preparation?
God promised the exiles a new heart to truly know Him, not just a return to their old circumstances. Are you seeking a change in circumstances more than a change of heart?
Zedekiah's remnant presumed on their apparent safety instead of turning to God. Where might you be resting in comfortable circumstances rather than genuine relationship with Him?
This vision shows God's favor does not follow visible appearances. Where do you need to trust God's assessment of your situation over your own?
The promise of a new heart here grows into the full new covenant later in Jeremiah. How have you experienced God giving you a new heart to know Him through Christ?

translationNotes.note: The deportation referenced is the first major exile under Nebuchadnezzar in 597 BC, distinct from the final fall of Jerusalem in 586 BC; this vision is set specifically in that earlier window.
'I regard as good' is a legal or evaluative declaration, not merely an observation; God is formally pronouncing His verdict and intention toward the exiles, not just describing their character.
'A heart to know Me' uses yada, a Hebrew verb for intimate, covenantal knowledge rather than mere intellectual awareness, the same relational sense used of a husband and wife 'knowing' each other.
